g.data-narration: Diagonalization simplifies matrix operations by transforming matrices into diagonal form. We cover when this is possible and the special case of orthogonal diagonalization for symmetric matrices.
A matrix A is diagonalizable if A equals P times D times P-inverse. The key condition is having n linearly independent eigenvectors, which is guaranteed when all n eigenvalues are distinct.
g.text: A is diagonalizable if A = PDP⁻¹ where D is diagonal and P is invertible.
Columns of P are linearly independent eigenvectors of A.
Diagonalization Condition: n linearly independent eigenvectors ⟺ diagonalizable.

g.data-narration: To diagonalize, first find eigenvalues from the characteristic equation. Then find eigenvectors for each eigenvalue. Form matrix P from eigenvectors and D from eigenvalues.
g.text: 3. Form P with eigenvectors as columns
4. Form D with eigenvalues on diagonal
If n distinct eigenvalues → always diagonalizable

g.data-narration: For matrix A equals three, one, one, three, eigenvalues are lambda equals two and four. Eigenvectors are one, negative one and one, one respectively.
For matrix powers, diagonalization makes computation easy. A to the power k equals P times D to the power k times P-inverse. D to the k simply raises each diagonal entry to k.
g.text: If A = PDP⁻¹, then Aᵏ = PDᵏP⁻¹ for any positive integer k.
Dᵏ is simple: just raise each diagonal entry (eigenvalue) to power k.

g.data-narration: Orthogonal diagonalization uses an orthogonal matrix Q instead of P. For symmetric matrices, A equals QDQ-transpose, where Q is orthogonal with orthonormal eigenvectors.
g.text: A is orthogonally diagonalizable if A = QDQᵀ where Q is orthogonal (Q⁻¹ = Qᵀ).
Spectral Theorem: Every real symmetric matrix is orthogonally diagonalizable.

g.data-narration: Key properties: symmetric matrices have all real eigenvalues, eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal, and Gram-Schmidt orthogonalizes eigenvectors when eigenvalues repeat.
g.text: Real eigenvalues | Eigenvectors for distinct λ are orthogonal | Orthonormal basis exists via Gram-Schmidt
For orthogonal Q: QᵀQ = I, Q⁻¹ = Qᵀ simplifies verification
